_meta.description: Bibliography evidence mapping for BKI (Belonging as Knowledge Infrastructure) framework. Maps each of the 4 BKI propositions and the Biocognitive OS / Broccolilly Equation to supporting bibliography entries. Entries marked PRESENT exist in the bibliography; entries marked MISSING are recommended additions.
_meta.bki_summary: BKI holds that belonging is the structural precondition for knowledge creation, transmission, and transformation. Governance frameworks delivered into low-belonging environments produce compliance theater, not behavioral change. HUMMBL creates the belonging infrastructure (transparency, receipts, trust scores) that makes AI governance adoption possible.

proposition_1_cognitive_scaffolding.claim: Belonging is a neurobiological prerequisite for higher-order cognition. Low-belonging triggers amygdala threat response, suppressing prefrontal cortex activity and preventing abstraction.

bibliography_entries.relevance: CRM (Crew Resource Management) training reduces error rates by creating psychological safety for junior crew members to challenge senior officers — direct empirical support for belonging as prerequisite for performance
Team effectiveness meta-analysis; identifies trust and mutual performance monitoring as prerequisites for adaptive behavior — behavioral analogue of belonging condition
Human-autonomy teaming; trust calibration affects cognitive engagement — low trust = cognitive disengagement, parallels low-belonging → prefrontal suppression

missing_entries.why: Edmondson's foundational study showing psychological safety predicts team learning behavior. The +0.35 SD effect size cited in BKI theory comes from this line of research.
missing_entries.citation: Edmondson, A. (1999). Psychological safety and learning behavior in work teams. Administrative Science Quarterly, 44(2), 350–383. doi:10.2307/2666999
missing_entries.priority: CRITICAL — add to T10 or T2

missing_entries.why: Walton & Cohen belonging uncertainty study: +0.3 SD academic performance via belonging intervention. The specific effect size quoted in BKI Proposition 1.
missing_entries.citation: Walton, G. M., & Cohen, G. L. (2011). A brief social-belonging intervention improves academic and health outcomes of minority students. Science, 331(6023), 1447–1451. doi:10.1126/science.1198364
missing_entries.priority: HIGH — add to T2 or T8

missing_entries.why: Lieberman 'Social: Why Our Brains Are Wired to Connect' — neurobiological basis for social threat/reward. Provides the amygdala/PFC mechanism claimed in Proposition 1.
missing_entries.citation: Lieberman, M. D. (2013). Social: Why Our Brains Are Wired to Connect. Crown.
missing_entries.priority: MODERATE — add to T6 or T2

proposition_1_cognitive_scaffolding.coverage_note: Behavioral analogues present (CRM, team effectiveness); direct neurobiological evidence missing. Edmondson is the critical gap.

proposition_2_relational_validation.claim: Knowledge claims gain authority through belonging networks, not logical validity alone. A correct framework delivered by an outsider is processed as threat, not knowledge.

bibliography_entries.relevance: Communities of Practice — belonging to the community of practice is prerequisite for knowledge legitimacy. Core support for Proposition 2.
Situated learning — knowledge is inseparable from the community context in which it is enacted. Authority flows through practice membership.
Legitimate peripheral participation — newcomers gain knowledge authority only through legitimate participation in community, not instruction alone.
Common ground theory — shared knowledge requires common ground established through belonging; expert-to-novice knowledge transfer fails without it.
SECI model — socialization (tacit-to-tacit transfer) requires shared experience and trust; ba (shared space) is a belonging-equivalent construct.

missing_entries.why: Bourdieu's habitus — the embodied dispositions that allow one to 'play the game' in a field. Proposition 2 is essentially a BKI restatement of symbolic violence: outsiders cannot have their knowledge claims recognized because they lack the field-specific capital.
missing_entries.citation: Bourdieu, P. (1977). Outline of a Theory of Practice. Cambridge University Press. doi:10.1017/CBO9780511812507
missing_entries.priority: CRITICAL — add to T2 or new T14

proposition_2_relational_validation.coverage_note: Strong coverage from situated cognition tradition. Bourdieu would add the sociological mechanism; absence noted.

proposition_3_embodied_knowing.claim: Wisdom transmits somatically through belonging relationships. Instructional delivery transmits propositional content only; it cannot reach embodied/habitual knowledge.

bibliography_entries.relevance: Reflection-in-action and knowing-in-action — tacit professional knowledge cannot be reduced to explicit rules; it is enacted through practice. Direct support for Proposition 3.
Distributed cognition — knowledge is embedded in practices, artifacts, and social relationships, not reducible to individual propositional content.
Distributed cognition framework — extends Hutchins; knowledge structure is distributed across people and environment, somatic dimension included.
Mental models and shared vision — organizational learning requires shared mental models developed through relational practice, not instruction alone.

missing_entries.why: Polanyi 'The Tacit Dimension' — the foundational source for tacit knowledge. 'We know more than we can tell.' Proposition 3 rests on this claim.
missing_entries.citation: Polanyi, M. (1966). The Tacit Dimension. University of Chicago Press. (No registered DOI; pre-DOI monograph.)
missing_entries.priority: HIGH — add to T1 or T2

missing_entries.why: Dreyfus and Dreyfus stage model of skill acquisition — expertise cannot be reduced to rule following; embodied practice is the necessary path.
missing_entries.citation: Dreyfus, H. L., & Dreyfus, S. E. (1986). Mind over Machine. Free Press.
missing_entries.priority: MODERATE — add to T2 or T6

proposition_3_embodied_knowing.coverage_note: Distributed cognition well-covered. Polanyi is the foundational gap; Schon partially compensates.

proposition_4_symbolic_systems.claim: Shared symbolic languages emerge FROM belonging structures, not the reverse. Imposing vocabulary before belonging inverts the causal arrow.

bibliography_entries.relevance: Reification and participation — symbols and shared language emerge from participation in communities of practice, not from top-down definition.
Organizational memory — shared interpretive schemes (analogous to symbolic systems) develop through organizational socialization, not instruction.
Externalization in SECI model — explicit symbols emerge from tacit knowledge through dialogue and metaphor; ba (belonging space) is prerequisite.
Transactive memory — shared encoding/retrieval systems develop in close relationships; shared symbolic structure is a relational artifact.

missing_entries.why: Vygotsky — zone of proximal development and the social origin of cognitive tools. Language and symbolic systems emerge through social interaction before becoming internalized. Foundational support for Proposition 4.
missing_entries.citation: Vygotsky, L. S. (1978). Mind in Society. Harvard University Press.
missing_entries.priority: HIGH — add to T2 or T8

proposition_4_symbolic_systems.coverage_note: Strong coverage from CoP and organizational memory. Vygotsky fills the developmental/linguistic origin gap.

biocognitive_os.claim: Humans shift through 6 cognitive modes depending on belonging conditions. Belonging determines which modes are accessible. Governance training delivered into threat-state environments is irretrievable.

bibliography_entries.relevance: Situation awareness levels 1-3 — under stress (low belonging), operators regress to Level 1 perception only; complex integration (Level 3) requires cognitive safety.
Trust calibration in human-autonomy teaming — over/undertrust corresponds to mode collapse in the Biocognitive OS model.

missing_entries.why: Porges' Polyvagal Theory — provides the neurobiological mechanism for the Biocognitive OS. Autonomic nervous system states (safe/danger/life-threat) directly map to the 6-mode framework.
missing_entries.citation: Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton.
missing_entries.priority: HIGH — add to T6 or T2

missing_entries.why: Flow state — optimal cognitive mode requires belonging-equivalent conditions (clear goals, feedback, challenge-skill balance). The Biocognitive OS Cognitive mode maps to flow.
missing_entries.citation: Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience. Harper.
missing_entries.priority: MODERATE — add to T3 or T8

biocognitive_os.coverage_note: Significant gap. The Biocognitive OS is a novel framework without direct bibliography support. Porges and Csikszentmihalyi provide the closest grounding.

broccolilly_equation.claim: S × T × I × C × D = R (Sovereignty × Trust × Integration × Clarity × Depth = Result). Multiplicative: zero in any factor yields zero result.

bibliography_entries.relevance: Team effectiveness meta-analysis identifies trust, leadership (sovereignty), and shared mental models (integration/clarity) as multiplicative factors.
Mental model alignment affects team performance multiplicatively — when alignment is zero, performance collapses regardless of individual competence.

missing_entries.why: Theory of Planned Behavior — provides the behavioral change multiplicative model that Broccolilly equation echoes. Attitude × norms × PBC = behavioral intention.
missing_entries.citation: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211. doi:10.1016/0749-5978(91)90020-T
missing_entries.priority: MODERATE — add to T2 or T8

broccolilly_equation.coverage_note: The Broccolilly Equation is a novel framework. Team effectiveness literature provides analogical support but no direct citation exists.

hummbl_governance_failure_thesis.claim: Governance frameworks fail not because of technical deficiencies but because they are delivered into low-belonging environments. HUMMBL creates the belonging infrastructure that makes AI governance adoption structurally possible.

bibliography_entries.relevance: CRM implementation failures in aviation were solved by creating shared mental models and psychological safety, not better checklists. Direct analogue for HUMMBL thesis.
Formal training programs fail to transmit practice; communities of practice succeed. Governance compliance theater vs governance internalization distinction maps here.
AI4People framework — identifies human/social prerequisites for AI ethics adoption; governance without human-centered foundation fails.
Internal AI auditing requires organizational belonging/trust infrastructure to function; audit findings without psychological safety are suppressed.

missing_entries.why: As above — the primary empirical support for why governance frameworks fail in low-safety environments.

hummbl_governance_failure_thesis.coverage_note: CRM and CoP provide strong empirical analogues. Edmondson is the critical missing direct citation.

critical_gaps: Edmondson1999PsychologicalSafety — psychological safety in teams (T10 or T2)
Bourdieu1977Practice — habitus and field theory (T2 or new T14)
Polanyi1966TacitDimension — tacit knowledge foundational text (T1 or T2)
high_priority_gaps: PorgesPolyvagalTheory2011 — neurobiological mechanism for Biocognitive OS
Vygotsky1978MindSociety — social origin of symbolic systems
summary.strongest_coverage_area: Proposition 2 (Relational Validation) — situated cognition tradition is well-represented
summary.weakest_coverage_area: Biocognitive OS and Broccolilly Equation — novel frameworks without direct bibliography anchors
